---
title: "Ratio and Proportion"
subtitle: "Comparison of two quantities, and what stays the same when you scale"
lesson-id: "t1.ratio-proportion"
tier: 1
prerequisites: ["t1.fractions-as-parts"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "ai-ml", "databases"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [Lesson 7 — Fractions](../07-fractions-as-parts/index.qmd)?
2. Can you compute $\tfrac{6}{8} = \tfrac{3}{4}$ — i.e. simplify a
   fraction by dividing numerator and denominator by their common
   factor?
3. Can you scale a recipe — for example, double the ingredients?
:::

## Touch

Two glasses. The left holds **water**; the right holds **lemon juice**.
Drag to set how much of each. The colour of the resulting "lemonade" on
the right shows the strength.

```{ojs}
viewof waterParts = Inputs.range([1, 12], {value: 4, step: 1, label: "Parts of water:"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof juiceParts = Inputs.range([1, 12], {value: 1, step: 1, label: "Parts of lemon juice:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const W = 720, H = 200, pad = 30;
  const total = waterParts + juiceParts;
  const juiceFrac = juiceParts / total;
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:760px;");

  // left: water glass
  const drawGlass = (x0, label, count, color) => {
    const gW = 100, gH = 130;
    svg.append("rect").attr("x", x0).attr("y", 30).attr("width", gW).attr("height", gH)
      .attr("fill", "#fff").attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 2);
    // Cells stacked
    const maxCells = 12;
    const cellH = (gH - 6) / maxCells;
    for (let i = 0; i < count; i++) {
      svg.append("rect")
        .attr("x", x0 + 3).attr("y", 30 + gH - 3 - (i + 1) * cellH)
        .attr("width", gW - 6).attr("height", cellH - 1)
        .attr("fill", color);
    }
    svg.append("text").attr("x", x0 + gW/2).attr("y", 22).attr("text-anchor", "middle")
      .attr("font-size", "13").attr("font-weight", "bold").text(label);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", x0 + gW/2).attr("y", 175).attr("text-anchor", "middle")
      .attr("font-size", "12").text(`${count} parts`);
  };
  drawGlass(pad, "Water", waterParts, "#90caf9");
  drawGlass(pad + 130, "Lemon juice", juiceParts, "#fff59d");

  // mixture glass
  const mixColor = `rgb(${Math.round(255 * (1 - juiceFrac * 0.4))}, ${Math.round(245 * (1 - juiceFrac * 0.05))}, ${Math.round(180 * (1 - juiceFrac * 0.5))})`;
  const x0 = pad + 320;
  const gW = 140, gH = 130;
  svg.append("rect").attr("x", x0).attr("y", 30).attr("width", gW).attr("height", gH)
    .attr("fill", mixColor).attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", x0 + gW/2).attr("y", 22).attr("text-anchor", "middle")
    .attr("font-size", "13").attr("font-weight", "bold").text("Lemonade");
  svg.append("text").attr("x", x0 + gW/2).attr("y", 175).attr("text-anchor", "middle")
    .attr("font-size", "12").text(`${total} parts total`);

  // ratio label
  svg.append("text").attr("x", pad + 510).attr("y", 80)
    .attr("font-size", "16").attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("fill", "#2c5f7c")
    .text(`Ratio (water : juice)`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", pad + 510).attr("y", 105).attr("font-size", "20").attr("fill", "#1f2933")
    .text(`${waterParts} : ${juiceParts}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", pad + 510).attr("y", 135).attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#666")
    .text(`Juice is ${(juiceFrac * 100).toFixed(0)}% of the mix`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

The **ratio** $4 : 1$ means: for every 4 parts water, 1 part juice. Try
$8 : 2$, then $12 : 3$. The lemonade looks (and tastes) the same.

## Wonder

1. Set $4 : 1$, then $8 : 2$, then $12 : 3$. The mixture colour stays
   the same. Why?
2. What if you set $4 : 4$? What does the mixture look like compared to
   $1 : 1$?
3. A recipe says "milk and flour in the ratio 2:5". You have only 200ml
   of milk. How much flour?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Set the ratio to **4 : 1**. Then **8 : 2**. Then **12 : 3**. The strength
of the lemonade did not change. What's going on?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each ratio reduces to the same simpler form.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4:1$ and $8:2$ and $12:3$ are all the same ratio — they all reduce to
$4:1$ (divide both sides by their common factor).

A **ratio** is just a fraction in different clothes:
$$\frac{\text{water}}{\text{juice}} = \frac{4}{1} = \frac{8}{2} = \frac{12}{3}.$$

Equivalent fractions (Lesson 7) and equivalent ratios are the same idea.
The "strength" depends only on the ratio, not the absolute amounts.
</details>

### Problem 2

A class has **boys and girls in the ratio 3 : 2**. The class has **30
students** in total. How many boys, how many girls?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The ratio means: out of every 5 students, 3 are boys and 2 are girls.
30 ÷ 5 = ?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Total ratio parts: $3 + 2 = 5$. So $30 \div 5 = 6$ students per "part".
Boys: $3 \times 6 = 18$. Girls: $2 \times 6 = 12$. Check: $18 + 12 = 30$. ✓

This is the standard "split a quantity in a given ratio" recipe: divide
the total by the sum of the ratio parts, then multiply by each.
</details>

### Problem 3

A recipe says **flour : sugar = 5 : 2**. You have **300 g of flour**. How
much sugar?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"For every 5 g of flour, you need 2 g of sugar." How many "5 g of flour"
go into 300 g?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$300 \div 5 = 60$ "units" of flour. Each unit needs 2 g of sugar. Sugar:
$60 \times 2 = 120$ g.

Equivalently: set up the **proportion**
$$\frac{5}{2} = \frac{300}{x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{2 \times 300}{5} = 120.$$
Cross-multiplying gives the same answer. Both methods are correct; pick
whichever feels natural.
</details>

### Problem 4

A map has scale **1 cm = 25 km**. Two cities are **8 cm apart on the
map**. How far apart in real life?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The map is just a *scaled-down* picture. Each cm is 25 km.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$8 \text{ cm} \times 25 \text{ km/cm} = 200$ km. Same idea as the recipe —
ratio scaling. Maps, blueprints, model trains, and game-engine
"world-to-screen" coordinates are all scale ratios.
</details>

### Problem 5

A snail moves **3 metres in 5 minutes**. At the same pace, how far does
it travel in **20 minutes**? In **1 hour**?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Find "metres per minute" first, then scale.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Speed: $3 \div 5 = 0.6$ m/minute.

In 20 minutes: $0.6 \times 20 = 12$ m.
In 60 minutes: $0.6 \times 60 = 36$ m.

A speed (or rate) is a ratio with a *unit* attached: m/minute,
km/litre, $/kg, beats/second. Every "per" expression in the world is a
ratio. We use the same arithmetic for "per litre" and "per minute" as
for "boys per girl" — that is the unifying power of the idea.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **ratio** $a : b$ compares two quantities. It is the same number as the
fraction $\dfrac{a}{b}$. Two ratios are **equivalent** if they reduce to
the same fraction:
$$a : b \ = \ k a : k b \quad \text{for any } k \neq 0.$$

A **proportion** is a statement that two ratios are equal:
$$\frac{a}{b} = \frac{c}{d} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad a \times d = b \times c.$$
This is **cross-multiplication** — the workhorse for solving "given
three of $a, b, c, d$, find the fourth".

A **rate** is a ratio of two quantities with different units (km/litre,
$/kg, m/minute). Every "per" expression is a rate.

:::

> *Two quantities in a ratio — multiply both by the same number,*
> *And the ratio holds.*
> *Set two ratios equal — cross-multiply to find the missing piece.*
> *Per-this-per-that is a rate, and a rate is a ratio in disguise.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A car travels **240 km on 16 litres of petrol**. At the same fuel
> efficiency, how far can it travel on **25 litres**?

A competent solver sets up a proportion:
$$\frac{240 \text{ km}}{16 \text{ L}} = \frac{x \text{ km}}{25 \text{ L}}.$$

Cross-multiply: $240 \times 25 = 16 \times x$. So $x = \dfrac{240 \times
25}{16} = \dfrac{6000}{16} = 375$ km.

**Answer: 375 km.** Or, equivalently: efficiency = $240 \div 16 = 15$
km/L, then $15 \times 25 = 375$ km. Same answer; same idea.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Reduce these ratios to simplest form: $6 : 9$, $\ 20 : 50$,
$\ 100 : 25$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2 : 3$, $\ 2 : 5$, $\ 4 : 1$. (Divide both sides by the largest common
factor.)
</details>

**W2.** Split **42** in the ratio $3 : 4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Total parts: $3 + 4 = 7$. Each part: $42 \div 7 = 6$. Result: $18$ and
$24$. Check: $18 + 24 = 42$, and $18 : 24 = 3 : 4$ ✓.
</details>

**W3.** A bicycle wheel makes **120 turns per minute**. How many turns in
**5 minutes**?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$120 \times 5 = 600$ turns.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A photograph is **6 inches wide and 4 inches tall**. To enlarge
it without distortion, you keep the **width : height ratio**. If the
enlarged width is **15 inches**, what is the new height?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Original ratio: $6 : 4$. Set up the proportion with the new width.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{6}{4} = \dfrac{15}{h}$. Cross-multiply: $6h = 60$, so $h = 10$
inches. The "aspect ratio" idea is the heart of every image, video,
and screen-design decision.
</details>

**P2.** A recipe for 4 people uses **300 g of pasta** and **2 eggs**.
You're cooking for **10 people**. How much pasta and how many eggs?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Find the *per-person* quantities first; then scale.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Per person: $300 \div 4 = 75$ g pasta and $2 \div 4 = 0.5$ eggs.
For 10: $75 \times 10 = 750$ g pasta and $0.5 \times 10 = 5$ eggs.

Recipe scaling is one of the oldest applications of proportion in human
history.
</details>

**P3.** **Percentages are just ratios with denominator 100.** Convert:
(a) $\tfrac{3}{4}$ to a percentage; (b) 0.85 to a percentage; (c) "30%
off" on an Rs. 1200 item — what is the discount?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Percent literally means "per hundred". $x\% = \tfrac{x}{100}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
(a) $\tfrac{3}{4} = \tfrac{75}{100} = 75\%$.
(b) $0.85 = \tfrac{85}{100} = 85\%$.
(c) $30\% \text{ of } 1200 = \tfrac{30}{100} \times 1200 = 360$. **Rs. 360
off**, leaving Rs. 840 to pay.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Two recipes for orange squash:

- Recipe A: **3 parts juice : 5 parts water**
- Recipe B: **2 parts juice : 4 parts water**

Which is stronger (more concentrated)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compute the *fraction of juice* in each total mix.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Recipe A: juice fraction = $\dfrac{3}{3+5} = \dfrac{3}{8} = 37.5\%$.
Recipe B: juice fraction = $\dfrac{2}{2+4} = \dfrac{2}{6} = \dfrac{1}{3}
\approx 33.3\%$.

**Recipe A is slightly stronger.** Many learners get this wrong by
comparing $3 : 5$ to $2 : 4$ part-by-part. The right comparison is the
fraction of juice in the total mix.
</details>

**S2.** A neural network learns by adjusting weights based on a *learning
rate* — typically a small ratio like $0.001$ or $0.0001$. What is this
expressing? Why does the choice matter?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
A learning rate is "how much to change the weight per unit of error". A
rate is a ratio with units.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The learning rate is the **ratio "weight change per unit of error"**. If
it's too high, weights jump around wildly and never settle. If it's too
low, training takes forever. Choosing it well — the field calls this
"hyperparameter tuning" — is one of the practical arts of machine
learning.

Behind every modern AI system is a careful set of ratios — gradient
scaling, regularisation strength, dropout rates, batch fractions —
each a Tier 1 idea applied at scale.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Ratio is the same as 'difference'."** No. A ratio of $4 : 1$ does
   not mean "4 more"; it means "4 times as much". The two ideas
   (additive vs multiplicative comparison) get confused all the time.
2. **"Compare ratios part by part."** As *Stretch S1* showed, $3:5$ vs
   $2:4$ doesn't yield to part-by-part. Always compute the *fraction of
   the total* (or equivalent decimals) and compare those.
3. **"Percentage is a different thing from a ratio."** Percent is just
   "per hundred" — a ratio with a fixed denominator. $25\%$ is the same
   as $\tfrac{1}{4}$ is the same as $0.25$ is the same as $1 : 3$ in
   "part : rest" form (careful: $1 : 3$ means 1 of every 4, not 1 of
   every 3 — see common error #1).
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you explain to a friend why $4 : 1$, $8 : 2$, and $12 : 3$ are
   the same ratio?
2. Without scrolling back, can you split 60 in the ratio $2 : 3 : 5$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(For W2-style three-term split: total parts $=2+3+5=10$, each part
$=60 \div 10 = 6$, so split is $12, 18, 30$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[Lesson 10 — Negative Numbers](../10-negative-numbers/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** — comparing two ratios *correctly* is genuinely
  hard.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P2 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Lesson 7 — Fractions](../07-fractions-as-parts/index.qmd)** — a
  ratio *is* a fraction with two named parts.

### What this unlocks

- **Tier 2 — Linear functions** — every linear relationship $y = mx$
  is a *constant ratio* between $x$ and $y$.
- **Tier 3 — Trigonometry** — the basic trig ratios (sin, cos, tan)
  are just ratios of triangle sides.
- **Tier 5 — Probability** — every probability is a ratio:
  favourable outcomes over total outcomes.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Every image, sprite, and screen has an *aspect
ratio*. Coordinate systems are linear maps — pure ratios. Physics
constants (gravity per second-squared, drag per velocity) are rates.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Learning rate (*Stretch S2*), batch size
ratio, train/validation/test splits, dropout rate, label-class ratios
in imbalanced datasets — modern ML is a thicket of carefully chosen
ratios.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** SQL aggregates yield ratios constantly: percentages,
counts-per-group, averages. The very query "the percentage of users
who clicked the link" is a ratio computation.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Currency exchange rates, mortgage interest rates,
nutritional labels (per 100 g), election results (vote shares), sports
statistics, fuel efficiency, exam scores out of 100 — the world is
governed by ratios.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (the lemonade widget makes ratio
  a *tasteable* property), Boole (the *why does the colour stay the
  same?* question precedes the equivalence rule), Russian school
  (S1 forces the part-by-part comparison error to be caught), Bhāskara
  (single-box Name + verse — the Indian tradition is the source of
  most of the cross-multiplication intuition for proportions, going
  back at least to Brahmagupta).
- **Self-study guarantees** all present.
:::
